title: Above the Print Shop
text: You meet reformers in a room above a print shop. They want to improve factory life and city health, but they need strong evidence before people will listen.
historyNote: Reformers used pamphlets, newspapers, petitions and public meetings to spread ideas and pressure leaders.
evidence: Reform movements needed evidence and public support to challenge industrial problems.

choices.label: Visit a factory to gather evidence.
Walk through crowded city streets.
Help prepare a petition.

title: Inside the Factory
text: The factory is loud and dusty. Children work close to moving machines. You note their ages, hours and the injuries workers describe.
historyNote: Evidence about child labour, long hours and injuries helped reformers argue for factory laws.
evidence: Factory evidence showed that children could face long hours, danger and poor health at work.

choices.label: Record workers' words exactly.
Add dramatic details to make the story stronger.
Ask workers if they belong to a union.

title: The Dirty Pump
text: In a narrow street, families share one water pump. Waste runs near the gutter. A mother says sickness spreads quickly in the crowded houses.
historyNote: Industrial cities often grew faster than clean water, drains and housing. Poor sanitation helped diseases spread.
evidence: Poor city sanitation made disease more likely in crowded industrial towns.

choices.label: Map the street and note where waste collects.
Interview families about sickness.
Ask the town council for cleaner water.

title: The Petition
text: You carry a petition through markets and streets. Some people sign quickly. Others fear angering factory owners or losing work.
historyNote: Petitions let ordinary people ask Parliament or local leaders for change. They were an important tool for reform campaigns.
evidence: Petitions showed that many people supported reform, even if they had little political power.

choices.label: Collect signatures outside the factory gate.
Speak politely with shopkeepers and families.
Take the petition to a local Member of Parliament.

title: Writing the Report
text: Your notes cover factory injuries, child labour, dirty streets and sickness. You must turn them into a clear report that people can trust.
historyNote: Reports and inquiries helped turn personal experiences into public evidence for reform.
evidence: Careful evidence made reform arguments stronger and harder to ignore.

choices.label: Use exact testimony and clear facts.
Print a short version for workers to read.
Add maps showing dirty water and crowded streets.

title: Workers Organise
text: Workers meet after dark to discuss wages, hours and safety. They talk about joining together so owners must listen.
historyNote: Unions grew as workers organised to improve pay and conditions. Early unions often faced suspicion or legal limits.
evidence: Unions helped workers act together instead of facing factory owners alone.

choices.label: Listen and record their main concerns.
Encourage peaceful organisation.
Warn them that owners may punish protest.

title: Public Health
text: A doctor explains that dirty water and poor drains spread disease. You realise factory reform is not enough if workers return to unsafe streets.
historyNote: Public health reform focused on clean water, drains, housing and disease prevention in growing towns.
evidence: Public health reform showed that industrial cities needed clean water, drains and better housing.

choices.label: Campaign for drains and clean water.
Send your sanitation evidence to Parliament.
Compare factory injuries with city sickness.

title: News from Parliament
text: A Member of Parliament reads your evidence. Some leaders support reform. Others say too many laws will hurt business.
historyNote: Parliament debated how much the government should regulate factories and cities. Reform often happened slowly.
evidence: Parliamentary debate shaped laws about factories, workers and public health.

choices.label: Argue for stronger Factory Acts.
Argue that public health must be included.
Seek support from unions and families first.

title: The Public Meeting
text: The hall is packed with workers, parents, shopkeepers and doctors. You speak about factory injuries, child labour, petitions and dirty streets.
historyNote: Public meetings helped build pressure for reform by spreading evidence and gathering support.
evidence: Public meetings helped turn local problems into wider reform campaigns.

choices.label: Call for limits on child labour.
Call for clean water and city sanitation.
Call for unions, petitions and Parliament to work together.

title: Ending: Reform Takes Time
text: Your work helps show why reform mattered. Evidence, petitions, unions, public meetings and Parliament all played a part. Factory Acts and public health reforms did not fix everything at once, but they began to change industrial Britain.
historyNote: Factory Acts gradually limited child labour and improved inspection. Public health reforms later improved sanitation, clean water and city planning.
evidence: Reform during the Industrial Revolution grew from evidence, public pressure, worker organisation and government action.
